title: One Small Thing Today. Just One.
subtitle: A three-minute activity for days when nothing feels possible
excerpt: A three-minute activity for the days when the depression or the exhaustion is so heavy that the to-do list cannot be looked at. Pick one small thing. Do it. That is the whole day.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The one-small-thing practice is a staple of writing on depression, on chronic illness, and on grief.

practice.whenToUse: Use on days when the depression, the exhaustion, or the long-term illness has made the normal to-do list unreachable.

content.text: A three-minute activity for the days when nothing feels possible. Pick one small thing. Do it. That is the whole day's plan.
New to activity as a practice? Read
Some days the to-do list is unreachable. The depression is heavy. The exhaustion is total. The chronic illness is loud. The grief has taken the steering. The normal day is not coming today.
On those days, the only workable plan is one small thing. The one small thing keeps a thread between today and your normal life. The thread matters even when nothing else does.
The body needs a small win on hard days. Without one, the day rolls over you and you go to sleep feeling worse. With one, the day has a small point of pride in it.
The small thing also keeps the muscles of normal life from going stiff. Over weeks of hard days, the one-small-thing keeps a path open back to the regular life.
Pick the smallest version of a normal thing. Brush your teeth. Open the curtain. Boil the kettle. Wash one bowl. Send one text. Step outside for one minute.
Pick it in the morning. Before the day has the chance to slip away.
Do it. The whole point is doing the one thing, not picking the right one.
Mark it. A small tick in a notebook, a smile to yourself. Say in your head: "That was the day's thing."
If anything else happens, that is a gift. The deal is one thing. Anything else is extra.
Original to homemade.education. The one-small-thing practice is a common one. It appears in writing on depression, on chronic illness, and on grief.
